retro_required_reason: user said 'ship it all' + 27-commit / 3487-line diff since v0.1.0 + new manifest permissions surface (declarativeNetRequest, *.s3.amazonaws.com host_perm) + new feature domains (threading, alerts, pinning)

what_went_right: Pre-tag review gate fired again at v0.1.33 and caught two real structural bugs the implementer missed: a thread+search filter intersection correctness bug (out-of-thread messages matching the query were leaking through) and a NOTIFICATION_REFS cache leak on auto-dismissed alerts. Both were fixed inline at 401ca0b before the tag, exactly mirroring the v0.1.0 cachedProxyTabId catch — confirming the gate is load-bearing, not bureaucratic.

Status badge correction at v0.1.26 (`live poll` + green dot instead of `disabled`) brought the UI back into truth-telling after a 10-commit window where the badge implied users had no live updates while polling was in fact delivering messages every 12s. The fix was small but the discipline matters — the runtime mechanism and the user-visible indicator have to move together.

declarativeNetRequest Referer rewrite at v0.1.18 was the structurally correct fix for the S3 image 403 problem (vs the speculative URL rewrite alone at v0.1.13). It required a new manifest permission + new rules.json, both clean and self-documented, and the legitimate path (substackcdn.com signed URLs) coexists with the rewrite rather than fighting it.

Flatten threaded replies + recursive walker (v0.1.20 `flattenReplies`) finally addressed the latent bug that polling had been silently dropping every in-thread reply since v0.1.11 — the Active rail and bell alerts had been frozen on each user's last top-level message for nine commits, and the in-code comment names the failure mode explicitly.

Issue templates + LICENSE + rewritten README (commit ba0507d) turned the repo from a private experiment into something an external user can file a bug against — the bug template explicitly asks for `[BetterSSC ...]` console tags, which is exactly the diagnostic signal the v0.1.3–v0.1.8 debug loop wished it had from the user.
what_went_wrong: v0.1.13 bundled five features in one commit (images + theme switch + slash commands + vi nav + WS retry) and produced a 7-commit fix tail through v0.1.19. The `g`-jumps-to-top keybinding alone was fixed three separate times (v0.1.13, v0.1.15, v0.1.19) because nobody walked the bundled feature end-to-end on a real chat before tagging. Same shape at v0.1.20 → v0.1.25 (pin + sort + flatten → thread badge placement + quote count + label rename, 4-commit tail).

WS got a second capitulation. v0.1.9 already fell back to polling after 5 fix commits. v0.1.13 retried it (bundled as 1/5th of a feature commit) with no new protocol evidence — just another speculative frame sequence. v0.1.16 disabled it AGAIN. That's two capitulations and one round-trip of wasted protocol effort. The retry was not gated on the missing piece (a side-by-side packet capture of a working native session).

Pin/bell icon design-color collision (member pinned-gold vs watched-green) was deferred into post-ship and paid off across FOUR sequential style commits: v0.1.29 SVG icons, v0.1.30 fill-state-when-on, v0.1.31 pinned-color-wins-over-watched, v0.1.32 watched-name-matches-bell-green. A 30-second design conversation before v0.1.20 (`when both states collide, which wins?`) would have collapsed all four into one.

Status badge lied for 10 commits between v0.1.16 (WS disabled) and v0.1.26 (badge rewritten to say `live poll`). Users who looked at the badge in that window would have concluded the extension was broken when polling was in fact delivering messages every 12s. The runtime change at v0.1.16 should have updated the indicator in the same commit.
three_whys: Why 1: The v0.1.11 → v0.1.33 range produced 23 commits across 4 hours where roughly half were small UI/correctness fixes for features shipped one or two commits earlier — and the same keybinding (g) and same status indicator (WS badge) were fixed multiple times.

Why 2: Because the two big feature commits (v0.1.13 with 5 features bundled, v0.1.20 with 3 features bundled) compressed five separate design passes into one think-through, so each feature got roughly 1/5th of the design attention it would have gotten in a one-feature-per-commit cadence. Bundled commits also obscure the cross-cutting interactions (vi nav vs theme vs slash commands all touch the keydown handler; pin state vs watched state both color the member name) so design-collision questions never get asked before shipping.

Why 3: Because the pre-commit walk-through is treated as a single gate per ship cut (the pre-tag review at v0.1.0 and v0.1.33), not a per-commit gate when a commit bundles user-visible UI surfaces. The pre-tag review catches structural bugs but it doesn't replace the 'walk each new UI surface on a real chat once' step that should fire whenever a commit changes user-visible behavior. v0.1.13 needed five walk-throughs and got zero; the seven follow-up fix commits ARE those walk-throughs, just spread across the next two hours instead of the next ten minutes.

evidence_citations.quote: feat(v0.1.13): images + theme switch + slash commands + vi nav + WS retry
feat(v0.1.20): pin users + sort active rail + flatten threaded replies
fix(v0.1.15): light theme default + g jumps to top + vi-active persists
feat(v0.1.19): per-user alerts + g scrolls to top + image cache + URL rewrite
fix(v0.1.31): pinned name color wins over watched + pin icon sits upright
fix(v0.1.32): watched user name color matches the bell (green)

Pinned name color must win over .member.watched, otherwise members who are BOTH pinned and watched end up purple (watch color) while pinned-only members are amber — that mismatch is confusing. The bell already carries the watch state visually, so the name leans on pin color exclusively.
fix(v0.1.16): silence 403s on images + disable WS attempt by default

WS is OFF by default in v0.1.16. Substack's wss://zyncrealtime protocol rejects our subscribe frames with `Invalid message` after auth OK, and we can't reverse-engineer the right shape from one-sided traces. To stop log noise and a wasted ~5s of open-then-error per page load, the attempt is gated behind a debug flag. Polling is the live mechanism.
fix(v0.1.26): status badge says "live poll" with green dot instead of "disabled"

v0.1.26: the badge now reflects the LIVE-update mechanism the app is actually using, not the raw WS state. When WS is "disabled" we're still very much live via polling — show that as a green "live poll".
fix(v0.1.33): pre-tag review fixes — thread+search intersection + notif leak

"retro_reasoning": "25 commits, 1700+ line diff, new manifest permissions, multiple feature areas, and 2 should-fix findings including a user-visible filter correctness bug", "critical_count": 0, "should_fix_count": 2
Drop the ref when the notification auto-dismisses or is swiped away without being clicked. Without this NOTIFICATION_REFS leaked entries for every alert that wasn't acted on.

When a thread filter is also active, INTERSECT — a group is shown only if it's both in the thread AND matches the query. (Without this, the text-search loop would overwrite the thread filter and any out-of-thread match would become visible.)
